content:
# Leviticus Chapter 19 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 69r–69v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_019_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_019_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Speak to the whole community of Israel and tell them: Be holy, because I — the LORD your God — am holy.

**3** "Every one of you is to honor his mother and his father. Keep my sabbaths. I am the LORD your God.

**4** "Do not turn to idols or make gods of cast metal for yourselves. I am the LORD your God.

**5** "When you offer a peace offering to the LORD, offer it in a way that you can be accepted. **6** It is to be eaten on the day you offer it, or on the next day. Whatever is left over by the third day must be burned up. **7** If any of it is eaten on the third day, it is tainted — it will not be accepted. **8** Anyone who eats it will bear the guilt of that offense, because they have profaned what is holy to the LORD. That person shall be cut off from their people.

**9** "When you harvest your land, do not harvest all the way to the edge of your field. Do not go back to pick up what the harvesters missed. **10** Do not strip your vineyard bare, and do not pick up grapes that have fallen. Leave them for the poor and for the foreigner living among you. I am the LORD your God.

**11** "Do not steal. Do not deceive one another. Do not lie to each other. **12** Do not swear falsely by my name — that would be profaning your God's name. I am the LORD.

**13** "Do not oppress your neighbor or rob him. Do not hold back a hired worker's wages overnight. **14** Do not curse the deaf, and do not put a stumbling block in front of the blind. Fear your God. I am the LORD.

**15** "Do not pervert justice. Do not show favoritism to the poor or deference to the powerful — judge your neighbor with fairness. **16** Do not go around spreading slander among your people. Do not stand by while your neighbor's life is at stake. I am the LORD.

**17** "Do not nurse hatred against your fellow Israelite in your heart. Confront your neighbor directly when he wrongs you, so that you do not share in his guilt. **18** Do not seek revenge, and do not hold a grudge against any of your people. Love your neighbor as yourself. I am the LORD.

**19** "Keep my statutes. Do not breed two different kinds of animals together. Do not sow your field with two kinds of seed. Do not put on clothing woven from two different materials.

**20** "If a man sleeps with a woman who is a slave and pledged to another man but has not yet been ransomed or given her freedom, there must be an inquiry — but they shall not be put to death, because she had not been freed. **21** The man must bring a ram as his guilt offering to the LORD at the entrance to the tent of meeting. **22** The priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering before the LORD for the sin he has committed, and his sin will be forgiven.

**23** "When you enter the land and plant any fruit tree, treat its fruit as forbidden. For three years it is forbidden to you and must not be eaten. **24** In the fourth year, all its fruit is to be a holy offering of praise to the LORD. **25** And in the fifth year you may eat its fruit. In this way your harvest will increase. I am the LORD your God.

**26** "Do not eat anything with the blood still in it. Do not practice divination or seek omens.

**27** "Do not cut the hair at the sides of your head or clip the edges of your beard. **28** Do not cut your body for the dead or mark yourself with tattoos. I am the LORD.

**29** "Do not degrade your daughter by making her a prostitute — or the land itself will turn to prostitution and be filled with wickedness.

**30** "Keep my sabbaths and have reverence for my sanctuary. I am the LORD.

**31** "Do not turn to mediums or spiritists. Do not seek them out, or you will be defiled by them. I am the LORD your God.

**32** "Stand up in the presence of the aged. Show respect for the elderly, and fear your God. I am the LORD.

**33** "When a foreigner lives as a stranger in your land, do not mistreat them. **34** The foreigner living among you shall be treated the same as a native-born Israelite. Love them as yourself — for you were foreigners in Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

**35** "Do not use dishonest standards in measuring length, weight, or quantity. **36** Use honest scales, honest weights, and honest measures — dry and liquid alike. I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt.

**37** "Keep all my statutes and all my ordinances and carry them out. I am the LORD."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Chapter That Is Everything

Leviticus 19 is, by any measure, the most remarkable chapter in the book and arguably one of the most ethically dense chapters in the entire Bible. It gathers into 37 verses an astonishing range of concerns: ritual purity, sabbath-keeping, gleaning law, wage justice, disability ethics, fair courts, honest commerce, care for foreigners, and — at its twin heart — two commandments that Jesus will name as the greatest in the Torah: "love your neighbor as yourself" (v.18) and, in the same language, "love the foreigner as yourself" (v.34).

The chapter opens with a command that sets the frame for everything that follows: *qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem* — "Be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy" (v.2). This is not a vague spiritual aspiration. The rest of the chapter defines what holiness looks like in practice, and it turns out to look like: leaving grain at the edges of fields, paying workers on time, not tripping the blind, standing up for the elderly, and loving foreigners. Holiness, in Leviticus 19, is irreducibly social.

### Verse 2: The Holiness Imperative

*Qadosh* — holy — is the central word of Leviticus and perhaps of the entire Pentateuch. Its root meaning involves separation, distinction, being set apart. God is holy because there is nothing else like God; the temple is holy because it is set apart for God; Israel is to be holy because it belongs to God. But this chapter immediately destabilizes any purely cultic understanding of holiness. The command "be holy" is followed not by ritual instructions but by ethical ones: honor parents, keep sabbaths, do not steal, do not oppress the poor. The chapter is making an argument: the character of God is expressed in the character of the community. To bear the name of the LORD is to become what the LORD is — not in essence, but in behavior, in the texture of daily life.

The rabbinic tradition called this chapter *parashat qedushah* — the portion of holiness. Rabbi Akiva called verse 18 ("love your neighbor as yourself") the greatest principle in the Torah. Hillel famously compressed the entire Torah into a negative form of the same verse: "What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbor — that is the whole Torah; the rest is commentary." Jesus agrees with Akiva (Matt 22:36–40) and pushes further: this commandment and the commandment to love God sum up everything.

### Verses 9–10: The Gleaning Laws — Welfare by Design

The gleaning legislation in verses 9–10 is among the most practically elegant welfare provisions in the ancient world. It does not create a bureaucratic redistribution system. It does not require the poor to apply to a central authority. Instead, it mandates an architectural gap in the harvest itself: the *pe'ah* (corner, edge) of every field and the *leqet* (fallen fruit, overlooked grain) of every vineyard are not to be claimed by the landowner. They are, by law, available to the poor and the *ger* (resident foreigner).

The system is brilliant in several ways:

1. It preserves the dignity of the recipient. The poor are not given charity in the modern sense — they must come and work the field themselves, gleaning what remains. They exercise labor; they are not reduced to passive recipients.
2. It distributes cost across the entire agricultural community proportionally: the larger your harvest, the more you leave behind.
3. It requires no administrative apparatus. The field is its own welfare mechanism.
4. It explicitly includes the *ger* — the foreigner. This is not an ethnocentric welfare provision. It is structurally open.

The book of Ruth is in many ways a sustained meditation on this law. Ruth's gleaning in Boaz's fields (Ruth 2) operates exactly within this legal framework, and the narrative turns on Boaz's faithful and generous observance of exactly these verses. Leviticus 19:9–10 is the law; Ruth is the story of what it looks like when that law is observed with grace rather than minimum compliance.

### Verse 13: Wages That Do Not Sleep

"Do not hold back a hired worker's wages overnight." This verse — *lo-talin pe'ulat sakir ittekha ad-boqer* — addresses a vulnerability that was acute in the ancient world and remains acute today. Day laborers are paid for survival, not for comfort. In a subsistence economy, a day's wage is a day's food. To withhold it overnight is not merely an inconvenience; it can mean a family goes hungry. Deuteronomy 24:15 makes the logic explicit: "Give him his wages the same day, before the sun sets, because he is poor and is counting on it."

The prophets return to this principle repeatedly as a mark of social justice. James 5:4 echoes it directly: "The wages you failed to pay the workers who mowed your fields are crying out against you. The cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord Almighty." The principle travels across the entire canon: economic power creates obligations, not just privileges. The employer who delays payment is engaging in a form of theft — using the worker's need as a source of temporary credit they never agreed to extend.

### Verse 14: The Deaf and the Blind — Foundational Disability Ethics

Verse 14 is one of the most quietly radical verses in the Torah: "Do not curse the deaf, and do not put a stumbling block in front of the blind. Fear your God."

The prohibition on cursing the deaf is striking precisely because the deaf person cannot hear the curse. The temptation being addressed is not the social harm of audible insult — it is the private pleasure of contempt toward someone who cannot defend themselves. The text assumes that the problem with cruelty is not merely its social consequences; it is the disposition of the heart. You cannot use another person's vulnerability as a site for your own gratification. The fact that the deaf person cannot hear does not make the curse less wrong — it makes the curser's contempt more naked.

The prohibition on the stumbling block before the blind is similarly focused on exploiting vulnerability. The blind person trusts the path. To place an obstacle in it is to betray that trust and weaponize helplessness.

The Talmud and rabbinic tradition expanded these prohibitions far beyond their literal scope. Any act that exploits someone's incapacity — selling bad advice to someone who doesn't know better, tempting someone to sin who lacks the discernment to refuse, giving a weapon to someone who might use it destructively — falls under the category of *lifnei ivver*, "before the blind." Disability becomes a metaphor for any situation of asymmetric vulnerability in which the stronger party owes protection rather than exploitation.

Both prohibitions close with the same phrase: *ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha* — "fear your God." The usual explanation is that these are private acts, performed when no human witness can hold you accountable. The appeal to divine fear is the appeal to the only court that sees everything. You may be able to hide your contempt from other people. You cannot hide it from the One to whom you will answer.

### Verse 15: Justice Without Favoritism

"Do not show favoritism to the poor or deference to the powerful — judge your neighbor with fairness." This verse is remarkable for its symmetry: it prohibits *both* forms of distorted justice. Favoritism toward the poor (*dal*) would seem to be the more sympathetic error — surely we should tip the scales toward the vulnerable? But the text says no. The poor person's poverty does not make them right. The court's job is truth, and sentimental solidarity with poverty is no more just than sycophantic deference to wealth.

The word for "judge with fairness" is *be-tsedeq tishpot* — "in righteousness you shall judge." *Tsedeq* is the same root as *tsedaqah*, often translated "righteousness" or "justice." Here it means something like: the judgment that corresponds to reality, that names what is actually the case. Justice is not about outcomes that feel satisfying; it is about correspondence between verdict and truth.

### Verse 16: The Anti-Slander and the Bystander

Verse 16 contains two prohibitions that belong together: "Do not go around spreading slander among your people. Do not stand by while your neighbor's life is at stake."

The connection between them is instructive. Slander (*rekhilut* — the word for a gossip, literally a "pedlar" who sells information) destroys people at a distance; the prohibition on standing by while someone's life is threatened requires active intervention. Together they define the obligations of belonging to a community: you may not harm through speech, and you may not opt out through silence. Both sins are failures of solidarity.

The second half of the verse — *lo ta'amod al-dam re'ekha* — is literally "do not stand on your neighbor's blood." The rabbis used this verse as the foundation for the entire duty to rescue: if you can intervene to prevent harm and do not, you are implicated. Contemporary legal discussions of bystander duty often invoke analogous principles. The Torah gets there first.

### Verse 17: Confrontation as Love

"Do not nurse hatred against your fellow Israelite in your heart. Confront your neighbor directly when he wrongs you, so that you do not share in his guilt."

The prohibition on "nursing hatred in your heart" is a prohibition on the simmering resentment that never speaks — the grudge that accumulates in silence while outwardly relations appear normal. This is a psychologically acute diagnosis: the most dangerous hatreds are not the ones that erupt into conflict but the ones that fester quietly, turning the one who harbors them bitter and corroding the relationship unseen.

The prescribed remedy is *hokhei'ach tokhiach* — an infinitive absolute construction meaning "you shall surely rebuke/confront." The doubling is emphatic: you have an obligation to say something. This is not permission to unload grievances self-righteously; it is a duty to preserve the relationship by naming the wound. The purpose clause is striking: "so that you do not share in his guilt." By remaining silent, you become complicit — both in the wrong itself (which might have been corrected) and in the ongoing damage to the relationship.

### Verse 18: The Second Greatest Commandment

*Ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha. Ani YHWH.*

"Love your neighbor as yourself. I am the LORD."

Five words in Hebrew, plus the divine signature. Jesus will name this, together with the Shema, as the entire Torah in miniature (Matt 22:36–40; Mark 12:28–34; Luke 10:25–28). Paul calls it the fulfillment of the entire law (Rom 13:8–10; Gal 5:14). James calls it "the royal law" (Jas 2:8). It is, by any measure, one of the most consequential sentences ever written.

A few notes on what the text actually says:

**Who is "your neighbor"?** The Hebrew *re'a* means, in its most immediate sense, "companion, fellow member of the community." In this verse, its natural referent is "your fellow Israelite" — the person who belongs to your people. This is important because verse 34 extends the exact same command — using the exact same language — to the *ger*, the foreigner. The chapter's structure is deliberate: verse 18 establishes the principle for the insider; verse 34 applies it to the outsider. Jesus' parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25–37) is an explicit expansion of this two-verse arc into a story that makes the outsider the model of the very love that the insider was supposed to show.

**What is "as yourself"?** The phrase *kamokha* — "as yourself" — is one of the most debated two-syllables in all of scripture. It does not say "more than yourself" or "instead of yourself." It uses the self as the measure. The implicit argument is that you already know, from the inside, what you need: food, safety, dignity, to be seen, to be treated honestly, to not be exploited. The command is to bring that knowledge to bear on your neighbor. The self is not erased; it is the standard of measurement.

**"I am the LORD."** The verse closes — as so many verses in this chapter do — with the divine signature *Ani YHWH*. The command to love is not a free-floating ethical principle derivable from reason alone. It is grounded in the character of the God who commands it. To love your neighbor as yourself is to participate in the life of a God who is, by nature, love.

The refrain *Ani YHWH* — "I am the LORD" — sounds sixteen times in this chapter (vv.3, 4, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 25, 28, 30, 31, 32, 34, 36, and twice implicitly in v.37's close). Each occurrence anchors the preceding law in the divine identity. The laws of Leviticus 19 are not a social contract between equals; they are the terms of a covenant with a God whose name has been revealed, whose history of faithfulness is on record, and whose character each law reflects.

### Verses 19–28: The Mixed Laws

The laws of verses 19–28 are sometimes called the *mishpatim* section of the chapter — the ordinances — as opposed to the ethical principles that surround them. They cover:

- Mixing prohibitions (v.19): no hybrid animals, no mixed seed, no mixed fabric (*sha'atnez*)
- A complex case involving a slave woman pledged to another man (vv.20–22)
- Fruit-tree laws and the *orlah* (first-fruits prohibition, vv.23–25)
- Blood-eating and divination prohibition (v.26)
- Haircut prohibitions (v.27) — likely related to pagan mourning rites
- Tattoos and body-cutting for the dead (v.28)

These laws resist tidy theological reduction, and that is, in part, the point. Holiness is not only a matter of the great ethical principles; it also involves the patient observance of particular practices that mark Israel as distinct — that shape the body itself as a site of covenant identity. The body that does not mix its fabrics, does not cut itself in mourning, does not eat blood, is a body that has internalized the separateness of the holy.

The fruit-tree *orlah* law (vv.23–25) is especially interesting. The word *orlah* means "foreskin" — the same word used for circumcision. A young tree's fruit is treated as *uncircumcised* for its first three years; in the fourth year, all its fruit is consecrated to God; only in the fifth year may the grower eat it. The parallel to circumcision is not accidental. Just as the child's foreskin is given to God on the eighth day as a sign of covenant dedication, the tree's firstfruits are given to God as a sign that the land and its produce belong to the LORD who gave them. The law links land, body, fruit, and covenant in a single gesture.

### Verses 29–32: Holiness in the Social Fabric

This section bundles several concerns that might seem unrelated but share an underlying logic: the protection of the social fabric against forces that corrupt it from within.

- Prostituting your daughter (v.29) — not merely a sexual prohibition but a warning about the land itself: a land where daughters are exploited for profit becomes a land saturated with *zanah* (prostitution, harlotry), which in Hebrew is also the metaphor for idolatry. The verse is cosmological: how a community treats its daughters shapes the moral atmosphere of the territory.
- Sabbaths and sanctuary reverence (v.30) — the refrain that appears also in 19:3, bracketing the chapter's central section.
- Mediums and spiritists (v.31) — the prohibition on consulting the dead is not merely about supernatural superstition; it is about the source of knowledge and guidance. Seeking wisdom from the dead is the inversion of seeking it from the living God.
- Standing for the aged (v.32) — the simplest law in the chapter and perhaps the most humanly recognizable. Rise when an old person enters the room. It is a gesture of recognition: this person has lived longer than you, has suffered and endured, and their continued existence is itself a form of testimony. The fear of God and the honoring of age are directly linked — the same posture of reverence that acknowledges God acknowledges human life made in God's image.

### Verse 34: Love the Foreigner as Yourself

*Ve-ahavta lo kamokha.*

"Love them as yourself."

The echo of verse 18 is exact and unmistakable. The structure of the command is identical: *ve-ahavta* (and you shall love) + object + *kamokha* (as yourself). The only difference is the object: in verse 18, *le-re'akha* (your neighbor/fellow Israelite); in verse 34, *lo* (him — the *ger*, the foreigner, the stranger).

The justification given is historical and experiential: *ki-gerim heyitem be-erets Mitsrayim* — "for you were foreigners in Egypt." Israel's identity as a people is grounded in the memory of vulnerability. You know what it is to be an alien — powerless, unprotected, subject to the whims of a dominant culture. That memory is not merely a fact about the past; it is a moral resource, a source of the empathy that love requires. You have been the foreigner. Therefore you know what the foreigner needs from you.

This verse, taken together with verse 18, defines the ethical architecture of the entire chapter: love extends without limit, inward to the neighbor and outward to the stranger, and the mechanism of that love is the imagination of the other's experience from within one's own.

The LXX renders this with characteristic faithfulness: *kai agapeseis auton hos seauton* — "and you shall love him as yourself" — the exact phrase that will appear in the Sermon on the Mount traditions and in Paul's letters. The verbal form *agapao* — to love with committed, covenant-keeping love — becomes in Greek the dominant term for the kind of love the New Testament will describe as the character of God (1 John 4:8). The chain of language runs directly from this verse through the LXX into the New Testament.

### Verses 35–37: Honest Measures, Honest Closing

The chapter ends as it should: with weights and measures. *Ephah* (dry measure), *hin* (liquid measure), the balance and the weight — all must be accurate. The Hebrew word is *tsedeq* — "righteousness" — applied to a measuring cup. The same word that describes justice in court and moral uprightness in character here describes a correctly calibrated scale. Justice is not only a grand concept; it is also the discipline of making sure your measuring cup says what it means.

The chapter closes in verse 37 with its broadest summary: "Keep all my statutes and all my ordinances and carry them out. I am the LORD." The final *Ani YHWH* — the sixteenth echo of the divine name — is the signature that both authorizes and grounds everything that has preceded it. These are not the statutes of a social contract or a political authority. They are the ordinances of the One who is.

### The LXX and Vaticanus (pp. 105–106)

Codex Vaticanus preserves this chapter with remarkable fidelity to the Hebrew. The LXX translators handle the ethical laws with precision, though some specific translation choices are worth noting:

The key term *ger* (resident foreigner) is rendered throughout as *proselutos* — "proselyte" — which in later Greek-speaking Jewish communities came to mean a convert to Judaism. This is a significant semantic shift: the Hebrew *ger* is not necessarily a religious convert; it is a social and legal category — a person of foreign origin living permanently within the community. The LXX rendering begins a narrowing of the term's scope that will continue in early Judaism. The New Testament's inclusion of Gentiles within the covenant community partly reverses this narrowing by returning to the broader horizon of the Hebrew term.

The *ani YHWH* refrain is rendered throughout as *ego kyrios* — "I am the Lord" — or occasionally *ego kyrios ho theos hymon* — "I am the Lord your God." The personal force of the refrain — its quality of direct divine address — is fully preserved in Greek.

The gleaning law (vv.9–10) is rendered with precise Greek vocabulary: *ametos* (harvest), *trygetos* (vintage), *staghylai* (grape clusters). The LXX was translated in a Hellenistic agricultural context where these terms would have been immediately recognizable to readers who lived in a similar economy.

Verse 18's *agapeseis ton plesion sou hos seauton* — "you shall love your neighbor as yourself" — is the exact form in which the verse enters the New Testament through Jesus, Paul, and James. The LXX here is not so much a translation as a transmission — carrying one of humanity's most consequential sentences intact from Hebrew soil to Greek-speaking ears to the ears of the entire world.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 69r–69v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 105–106 (LXX).*
